speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor, Michael Kaplan. Date, February 7, 1995. Tape four. You were saying, Mr. Kaplan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After work, we used to go out. And-- and our friends were only German soldiers. So this way, the suspicion, they could not suspect us that we are anybody else but a German. But as we worked and everything went normal, the Russian front started to come closer by. The-- the-- the German officer then in charge of that plant decided that it's time to pack it up and move back to Germany. So he started to take out the best equipment and send it back to Germany. Towards the end, he told us-- with the last train, he told us that he would like us to-- take us with him. And he's going to put up another factory there, and we're going to work there. Meantime, we-- we didn't have no-- I mean, we didn't have no-- no-- no appetite to go back into Germany. We-- we spoke to the girls already that we would like to escape and-- and stay here. Everybody had a girl. A steady girl, we're talking about now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the day came that we're supposed to-- while-- while he was telling us, we-- we got-- through the girls, we got in contact also with the partisan group-- not the partisan group. Through one of the girls' father, he used to be an old communist. Him, you-- we-- we could trust. So we told him that we are Jews, we are Jewish. And we would like to remain here and if he can arrange for us some transfer into the partisans. So the word came back that, since you're willing to work with us, you're more valuable to be on the-- on the-- in the plant, since the front comes-- comes towards-- towards this town, and to give them information that they would require. So the information they ask-- because in this plant where we worked, we used to fix tanks, trucks, all the paraphernalia from the-- from the front. And the soldiers, when they came in a truck or a tank, the crew came-- came with them. They remained with the tank as long as we fix it, and then they went back to the front. So while we worked with them, we could find out what-- when did they leave Germany, how many was in the company, where did they fight, what kind of fight, what the moral situation is, mental situation in the German Army. All these things we relayed to-- to that-- we-- we had a girl that did-- did the contact with the other guy. Because he couldn't expose himself. But he was-- probably he was, since he was a communist in the-- in the-- in the old days, he couldn't be-- he couldn't move around too much. So anyway, that girl took the message for-- forth and back. And there was once a message that we-- they're looking for boots. They need boots for the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since we knew that guy, that Hans, where we got the uniforms, we went over to him. And we knew that he-- he likes to drink. He was the only one in that place. So we took a bottle of schnapps, and we took some salami.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we told Hans, look, we need about 10 pair of boots. Can you give it to us? We have friends in town that need it. They want to change. OK. So he got the boots. We-- she-- she picked up the boots, one by one, little by little. And we got into it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and-- and basically, they asked us to be in place. There's another thing. The factory, [BACKGROUND NOISE] it start moving out just in a hurry, so to speak. And the Germans, whenever they-- they left anything of value, they used to dynamite and blow it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they asked us to watch where they're putting the dynamite, in what places they're putting-- putting it on. So there, in case they send over a platoon or somebody that could dismantle, they know where to go to look for it. So, this-- this type of information-- which was very valuable to them. So the last day, we came in to-- to-- to the-- to the factory. He said, tomorrow, you bring your stuff. And we are leaving tomorrow evening. Because on the other side of the-- of the river, the Russians were there. They were staying-- staying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I have to tell you, too. The Russians were making up a fabricated bridge under the water. The-- at nighttime, they used to send in the-- the divers. And they were putting together the bridge. The bridge was about 4 or 5 feet below water. The Germans didn't suspect that. They didn't know that was going on. So they was making in the forest that bridge. And when the attack came, all of a sudden-- they made three bridges, I think, this area, yeah. All of a sudden, tanks are walking over the water. The Germans were running like rats. They-- they didn't expect. Because the water was maybe-- maybe 6, 7 meters deep. No tank could cross over. Anyway, so they were running like rats. And they-- they cross over into town. The-- the Russians occupied the town. And since we were Germans to other people, everybody pointed their finger at us. We are Germans, so they-- they arrested us. And they took us then to the KGB. And the KGB [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put us in a basement. And everybody was in a different room. So they-- they started to interrogate me. I know about me. They interrogate me, said, I had to stay straight. They tell me, you were spies, and you this, and you that. I said, no, we are Poles. I didn't say we were Jews. We are-- we are Poles. And we are-- the Germans took us to-- to work. We had no choice. This is the first chance we had. We-- we-- we escaped. And we helped. We tried to help the partisans. We helped the partisans, you know. We gave them boots. We gave them information, everything. You're lying. You're lying. You know, he's screaming and yelling. You're going to be shot. Put me back in the basement. Then he took out the other guy. And all three of us told the same story. While he was telling the story, next day, the people found out that we are arrested. Oh, there's another thing. Since we're pressed for time, I cannot tell-- tell the story. We were-- there were about 800 Russians were hiding underground in town. And they didn't have what to eat. And where we worked, when we escaped from-- from the train-- I'm trying to skip over. [LAUGHS] It's-- when we escaped from the train, we worked in a flour mill. The train went away. And we escaped from the train. We worked in a flour mill, because we thought every day the Russians would come over. While we worked in the flour mill, we told them that we are Poles, to that-- to that captain over there. He was a captain, who was running the flour mill. So by then, they knew that we are Poles. We told everybody that we are Poles. Although, we wore German uniforms, which was very common, you know. The Poles would volunteer into them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us, look here, you are Poles. Would you like to help us out? They had some 800 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And potatoes were covered with ground. And when the Germans, before they left, they took out from the ground the-- the potatoes. And the people were hiding inside there. In the-- because the Germans chased out all the people from-- from-- from the town. So we had a task to give them flour and all kind of things that we could. We cooked up and-- and-- and-- fed these people. And all of a sudden, we're finding out there's three places of them. And in the factory-- I mean, in the flour mill, there were Ukraine people also hiding there. So anyway, we-- we did our best. We fed them and everything. And when the Russians came in, they arrested us. So when this captain started to interrogate me, so I told him, look here, there are people here to-- to testify that we are not the Germans. We-- we are Poles. We helped them. And he took me in the basement. And since everybody told him the same story, next day, all these people in the front of that KGB were writing up a-- a list, signed their signature that we are good Poles. And we helped them, and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he comes in and said, look here what the people did for you. He said, I had to let you go. And he let us go. And, oh, we were heroes there. We were heroes. And that's how we were-- we were liberated. Then it was a time before. When-- when-- after that-- in between that, they put us with German-- German prisoners, also to find out what's what. There in nighttime, they took us out-- they took three of us out. And there was a lieutenant, a-- a-- a KGB man. He asked, are you Jews? We said, no, we are Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. Don't fool me. I'm going to make you put your pants down, and I'll find out right away. So we told, yes, we are Jews. They said, look here. Now, listen to this. Lieutenant that works-- worked in the KGB on the front line, he said, keep your mouth shut. Go on like-- like Poles, because they don't like us here either-- in those days. So we knew how to conduct ourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we were liberated. And-- the-- the-- everybody signed. There was a big party. And we were heroes over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- and we started to work again [? in the-- in the-- ?] in that factory there. Because this is the only place that you worked. And all the doors were open for us. And then after a while-- after a while, they were conscripting people into the army. So they took us in, too. Because we were eligible and everything. So they took us into the 47 Russian Division. Since we spoke a good German and a good-- a good Russian, they wanted to know what's going on on the German side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night, they-- they told me, look here. We're going to send you over on the German side to find out what's going on. Then he comes back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, we have problems with our scouts. They're going there, and they're laying down and sleeping. And they don't bring any-- any important news. So I said, fine. Whatever you want, I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me over with this scout, with a separate-- separate group, scouts. And they took me over there. And they put me in a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was told that this group has to pass the other group. The real scouts had to pass through here. And you tell us if that's what happened-- what happened. And towards the evening, they come and pick me up. And this is what happened. The scouts went there. They find-- they found out what-- where the Germans are, what kind of armament they have. And they came to pick me up. But after a while, the Polish Army start to be organized. The-- it was established a Polish government already [INAUDIBLE]. And the Polish Army started to be organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, look here, you're Polish citizens. You have to go to the Polish Army. So I says, OK, fine. Polish Army, I felt, you know, a little bit easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, boy, [SIGHS] I would never go if I knew what-- what happened there. Such an anti-Semitism. The Jews were berated. They were-- unbelievable, on Russian soil. They were eating Russian food. They were fed and armed by the Russians. And-- and still, the-- the Jew was something that should not exist anymore. Let me tell you one thing. Katyn-- probably, you read about Katyn. There, Stalin killed 11,000 or 12,000 Polish officers. There were a few Jewish officers, too. Not too many, you couldn't become a Polish officer in-- in the Polish Army. But bless-- and the Germans discovered that. So Stalin said the Germans did it. And the Germans said Stalin did it. But whoever did, bless his hands that he killed these fascists, these anti-Semites. Because if these people would have been in the army, the Polish Army, I would've been alive. How many times people-- we were going with the trucks when we already joined the Polish Army. We were taking-- since we were drivers, we were mechanics, they put us in a truck company. So-- so the-- the-- we were taking to the front food and ammunition. From the front, we used to take wounded soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the soldiers used to tell me, when the fire across-- a strong fire came across from the-- from the German side, they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], go up in the tree and see where the fire is coming from. You don't do that. And anybody has a little bit of brains, you stick your head into the mud. That's why-- you know, that's why I say blessed should be the-- the people, the government, that killed off these-- these anti-Semites. Do you remember what year it was that you joined the Polish Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '4-- '44, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: '44? Do you remember what time of year it was? What the weather was like, perhaps?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summertime, summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you think it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were bathing in one-- in one-- in one place. And we had to run to the open ground and get the uniforms over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- at that point, did they offer you any training in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They kept us-- our group, they kept us for two weeks. We should go into the officer's school. So I saw with whom I'm dealing, I said, no, I don't want to be an officer. I want be a soldier. I don't have any responsibility for these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting there for two weeks. [LAUGHS] And one day, came a Russian-- because in the Polish Army was from lieutenant up, all the command was Russian in Polish uniforms. So one day, a-- a captain came up with a Jeep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he forgot that we are-- we are three qualified mechanics. Without anything, without going in to get the permission to take us away, [LAUGHS] he took us out in the Jeep and drove us away into the transport company. So he got himself a bargain. He had mechanics and good drivers that were reliable. But while we were going-- when the war came to an end, we had to go long trips. So what happened, the-- the-- the drivers and the soldiers, we slept over sometimes in a little town, in order, you know, to rest and go further. These guys used to sip out-- take out the gasoline from the-- from the-- from the-- from the trucks and sell for vodka. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, the middle of the road, you're finding out this guy's stuck, because has no gasoline. So I asked him, well, what happened to the gasoline? Eh, they said, dah, dah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time there used to be a convoy from 50 or 100 trucks, they put me in charge. Because they knew I will get 'em back. Otherwise, the guys would get stuck on the road. One-- one-- one-- one case was we drove into a forest. And all of a sudden, the Germans tried-- tried-- shooted-- throw in artillery there. And everything was going upside-down. And we were-- we were in a bunker. While the artillery was-- evidently, while the artillery was going on, some of these soldiers took mine cover from the-- from the truck. And he traded in-- he wanted to trade it for vodka, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got out after the-- the-- the artillery stopped. And I look at my truck, says, this isn't my truck, or not? The [NON-ENGLISH] is missing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the cover is missing. I looked around on other trucks, there's mine. So I took mine, and I took his.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you go back to the company and something missing, you're going to pay for it, or you go to jail. So when we come back to the company, before going into the company, I said, look here, pick up your-- your-- your thing. What you wanted to do to me? Now, I'm not a pig, as you. I'm giving you back, so you won't have any problems when you come back to the company.

speaker: interviewee
text: These-- these things happen. [INAUDIBLE] You know, if you are taking these soldiers to-- to the hospitals on the way back from the-- from the front and many of them-- we had a case where we were standing in 19-- what is it-- in '45, December-- November, '45. We were on the-- on the Weser River. I mean. November, '44. Pardon me. On the Weser River, it was all frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were Polish soldiers with a white flag hidden in that. They want to surrender. So what did the general do? He have them lay-- three of them or four of them were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had them lay down on the ground, and a tank run them over. That's a death sentence. On the front, the general was-- was [LAUGHS] your judge, and jury, and executor-- executor, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, you must have had some type of news from the outside world of what was going on in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. Whenever we liberate a Polish town, I used to ask them, what-- where are the Jews? What happened to the Jews? Where are the Jews? They said, we don't know. The Germans took them away. Everybody had the same excuse. They don't know. But they had-- they had good stuff. And you-- you came into their house, they have good stuff. We see it's not there. A peasant couldn't have a piano or other things like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we went-- like, we liberated Majdanek, Lublin-- Lublin. And then, you know, I don't have to tell you how the concentration camps looked after-- Majdanek was a-- was a-- a death camp. This is not a labor camp. It was a death camp. There, you got in, you come out just through the chimney. We liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, you know, around Majdanek, we-- in the free time-- you know, we had some time on our own-- we used to go out to Prague. Prague was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of Warsaw. So we used to go. And there we got to know Polish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to have, you know, on my truck, in the back of my truck, what I took from the Germans. Not from the Germans, physically, but from in the apartments, what they left, pillows, and cases, and covers, those kind of things. So I figured I didn't need it any more. I gave it to them. And they need some transportation, so I took them here and there. Because we-- once you're in the truck, you're the boss. If you're by yourself, not if you go with a-- with a convoy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they robbed the Jews left and right. But they didn't say that they killed the Jews. In a-- in a-- in a way, they helped the Germans kill the Jews. Because if a Jew escaped, they got a kilo of salt, or they got a bottle of vodka. So the Jewish people were-- not too many Jews remain alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we liberated Warsaw, I saw only three people walking, three people. This was in January, 17, 1945. Three people we met. We couldn't stay in Warsaw. We're passing by and going-- going further. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nobody did-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] did confess that they helped the Germans to-- to round up the Jews or-- or-- or kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any medals or military decorations when you were in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have six of them, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that led to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Circumstances-- you behave good. You did your duty. That's why you got the medal. And some-- some-- I never wrecked my truck. Some people, they went for a ride. They came back with the ripped off-- you know, somebody got stuck there and never got back, because he couldn't help himself. With the convoy that I- that I was assigned with, or I went by myself, I never-- never left nobody behind. Or, I took him in tow, or we give him gasoline. In tow, you take only when the motor-- something wrong with motor. But the others, you give them gasoline. He drives on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you take a little from this truck, from the other truck. Not everybody was stealing and selling gasoline, so you divide it. You have to use some-- some sort of a common sense. So this-- this type of-- you know, that's why they gave you the medals. When did you end your military service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you end your military service? When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, enter?

speaker: interviewer
text: End. When was it over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was over in January, '46. No, no. February, I think, 1946, I was de-mobilized.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then that's a problem. Then I looked for a-- for Jewish people. Where did they go? There was no shul that, you know-- this was the point where all the Jews congregated. So I found out that in Prague-- I was de-mobilized in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found out that in Prague, there's a place, a Jewish restaurant. So I went there. And there were plenty of Jewish people. I found Jewish people there. And everybody had his-- had his own story, you know? Was this the first time you actually knew what had happened to the people in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Majdanek, did you actually know at the time that you were at Majdanek that everybody was leaving through the chimneys? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were plenty of people laying around dead. But if you're dead, you don't look if the guy is circumcised or not. Or in the case with women, they're all naked laying around dead. So you couldn't, you know, make any different-- you couldn't draw any conclusions that these people were Jewish or something. But the people that escaped, got through the war in the forest or the people that remained alive after concentration camp, they told me the stories what-- what really happened, how the Poles helped them, how the Poles sold them out to the Germans. This is how I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a-- when I came to that restaurant, they had a committee in those days already, a committee of the survivors. So I went up to the committee. And I said, I want to send a telegram. So they took me a telegram to my sister, to Israel, that I am alive. And I knew that my brother in [INAUDIBLE] was working the [INAUDIBLE], he's alive, too. Because he-- at the last minute, I had correspondence with him. And he said that they gave him a paper that he cannot be taken to the army, because he's too valuable. He was an armature winder. So these people, they didn't take to the army. So I knew that he, for sure, he's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you next see your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I met my brother in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he came to the States. Yeah, when he came to the States. He wasn't in Israel. I had no opportunity. I had no money to go to-- to Israel to see him. And so when he came to the States, I sent him out papers. You know, on this-- [INAUDIBLE] a relative, was easier for him to come to the United States. And I was already established, so he came. And that's how I met my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else about the Holocaust or messages for future generations about what you've learned from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say one thing. If the war would have lasted another six months, I wouldn't have been here. It was impossible, because they-- they became so efficient in-- in the extermination, catching Jews, that you couldn't get. If anybody remained alive, he would have remain a Pole or anything else with intermarriages or something like that. And second, I would like to give a message that, if ever happened a Holocaust in the United States, people should arm themself and-- and fight to the last. Because if-- it was the case in Poland. It wasn't the partisans. And the partisans, if the Germans knew that these people have arms, they were very careful. They didn't go like to a [INAUDIBLE]. In Poland, the Poles drag old Jews, old women, they drag them out from-- from under the ground, so to speak, in order to give them to the Germans. Because they got a price for it. But if now, anything should happen, any Jew, if he does not arm himself, is-- is lost. I think this should be the-- the lesson. You know, like there's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an American philosopher, George Santiago said, people that don't remember history, history will repeat itself. That's so true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you time and for your interview, Mr. Kaplan.
